OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: Honestly, I've never been on social media myself, and honestly that's probably why I don't lose sleep over fake content like so many people do. But I see it all the time at the salon. My clients come in stressed out, showing me things they've seen online that got them all worked up, and half the time it turns out to be completely made up or taken out of context. As someone who works with people every single day, I've learned that the best defense is just staying grounded in what's real and tangible. When my clients bring me these stories, I try to help them think critically about what they're seeing. I ask them questions like, where did this really come from? Who benefits from people believing this? Does this match what I actually know about how the world works? It's the same approach I use when I'm learning new hair techniques or staying informed about things that matter to me. You have to go back to trustworthy sources. For me, that means talking to people I know and trust, reading from established news outlets, and not just accepting the first thing I see. I think a lot of the anxiety people feel comes from the constant stream of information without any filter. That's why I'm actually grateful I'm not caught up in that world. My kids are growing up with these platforms though, and I'm trying to teach them to be skeptical, to ask questions, and to remember that not everything online is real or worth their emotional energy. The truth is, fake content thrives because people are anxious and looking for answers. My Hindu faith teaches me about truth and living authentically, and I think that applies here too. We need to be intentional about what we consume and share, and we need to remember that real connections and real information come from real relationships and real sources. It's not complicated, just requires a little bit of mindfulness.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: This persona is a 44-year-old hairdresser with no social media presence, high neuroticism (78), low openness (25), and low conscientiousness (10), but moderate agreeableness (46). Her lack of social media use is the defining characteristic that shapes her perspective. Her high neuroticism suggests she's prone to anxiety, but her absence from social media protects her from constant exposure. Her low openness and conscientiousness suggest she prefers practical, straightforward approaches rather than complex systems. Her moderate agreeableness and role as a hairdresser (a people-facing profession) inform her empathetic but grounded perspective. Her Hindu beliefs about truth and authenticity provide a philosophical foundation. She would approach this topic from the angle of someone observing others' struggles with fake content rather than experiencing it directly, with practical wisdom gained from daily conversations with clients.
response_parsed.tone: Pragmatic, empathetic, slightly detached but caring, grounded in personal experience and observation, conversational and warm without being overly enthusiastic, honest about her own limitations while offering practical wisdom from her unique vantage point.
